straight_beat.name: Raymond 'Ray' Tortorelli
straight_beat.column:
The Yankees lost again. Not in a way that makes sense, not in a way that feels fair, but in the way that has become all too familiar this season. A 1-0 defeat to the A’s, a team that plays the game like it’s the final out of a World Series. The Bronx box, which has held so many triumphs over the years, now feels like a cage.  

The bullpen, that fragile line of defense, continues to haunt us. Cole pitched into the seventh, as if he were trying to make up for the innings he missed last season. But even a seven-inning performance isn’t enough when the offense can’t find the barrel. The Yankees have the talent, the payroll, the history — but tonight, they looked like a team that’s lost its rhythm. The A’s, for all their mediocrity, played with urgency. They didn’t take the game for granted. That’s the difference.  

I’ve covered this team for decades, seen them rise from the ashes of the 1994 strike, rebuild in the ’90s, and finally break through in ’96. Back then, the Bronx box was a place of destiny. Now,

optimist.name: Franca 'Frannie' Bellino
optimist.column:
The Yankees lost yesterday, but let’s not let that sour the day. A 1-0 decision? That’s the kind of game that makes you grab a pastrami sandwich and say, “Ah, but the season’s still young, pally.” You know what I saw from Juan Soto yesterday? October in his eyes. By the time the leaves turn we’re having a parade, and I’m catering it.  

Now, I’ll be the first to admit, a loss stings. Especially when you’re looking at that standings board and see the Yankees in the hole. But here’s the thing—this team isn’t just about the old guard. Look at the kids in the rotation, the bullpen, the farm system. They’re not just prospects; they’re the future. And the future? That’s where the parade starts.  

Take the young arms, the ones with fire in their veins and a fastball that

pessimist.name: Sol Hirschberg
pessimist.column:
The Yankees lost again. Not in a dramatic way, not with a meltdown or a meltdown of the bullpen, but in the most frustrating, insipid manner possible: 1-0 to the Athletics. A team that’s not even in the playoffs. A team that’s been out of the playoffs for five years. A team that’s been out of the playoffs for five years. And here we are, 8-4, two games behind in the AL East, with the same old excuses. Hal is not George, but that doesn’t mean Cashman should be handing out contracts like it’s 1998.  

Let’s talk about the game. The Yankees didn’t hit. They didn’t field. They didn’t even make a decent play. The Athletics, for all their mediocrity, managed to string together a few hits and a couple of runs. Meanwhile, the Yankees’ offense looked like a team that’s been told they’re good enough to win but not good enough to actually win. The pitching? Well, the starters were okay, but the bullpen? The bullpen is the same as it’s been for years. A bunch of guys who can get out of jams but can’t close them. And when they’re down a run in the ninth, you know what happens?
